1: "The prisoner of Christ Jesus for the sake of you Gentiles" — Paul writes from prison (most likely Rome, c. AD 60-62, though Caesarea and Ephesus are also proposed). In Roman honor-shame culture, imprisonment was a mark of defeat and disgrace; a prisoner's clients would be expected to feel shame by association. Paul inverts this: his imprisonment is "for the sake of" the Gentiles, reframing it through the patron-client framework as honorable suffering on behalf of those he serves. The formula "prisoner of Christ Jesus" (not "prisoner of Rome") asserts that his real captor is Christ — Rome holds his body, Christ holds his allegiance and purpose.
2: "The administration of God's grace" — oikonomia (stewardship, household management) is a technical term from Greek household and estate administration. The oikonomos (steward) managed a great household's resources on behalf of the master, with delegated authority and significant responsibility. Paul presents himself as the steward of a divine household secret: he did not invent the mystery of Gentile inclusion but received it as a trust from the divine householder and must administer it faithfully. The term also carries the sense of an ordered plan or dispensation.
3: The contrast with mystery-religion esotericism is implicit but important. In the mystery cults (Eleusinian, Dionysian, Mithraic), initiates underwent preparatory stages before receiving secret knowledge, and they were forbidden on pain of divine punishment from revealing the content to outsiders. Paul's mystērion follows the Jewish apocalyptic tradition (Daniel 2:18-19; 1 Enoch Ephesians 103:2; 1QpHab) where raz (mystery) is a divine secret disclosed to selected seers — but disclosed precisely so it can be proclaimed. Paul's mystery is the opposite of esoteric: it is now openly announced to all.
4: "When you read" acknowledges that this letter is being read aloud in the gathered assembly — the standard mode of reception for both epistolary and scriptural texts in antiquity. Ancient readers were auditors, and texts were composed with this oral reception in mind. The act of reading together is itself a participation in the unveiling of the mystery Paul describes.
5: "Not made known to people in other generations as it has now been revealed" — this does not claim the OT was entirely silent about Gentile inclusion (the OT prophets anticipated it: Isaiah 49:6; Ephesians 56:6-8; Amos 9:11-12). The point is that the specific form of that inclusion — Gentiles as co-heirs without becoming Jews, on equal terms through Christ — was not previously disclosed. The full reading of the OT's Gentile horizon was not available until the Christ-event provided the hermeneutical key to unlock it.
6: The three compound "together-with" words Paul coins here have no precedent in Greek or Jewish literature: synklēronoma (co-heirs), syssōma (members-together-of-one-body), symmetocha (co-participants in the promise). The novelty of the coinage signals the novelty of the reality. Jewish proselytes were historically incorporated into Israel by adopting its practices and identity; Paul's mystery is co-heirship without assimilation — Gentiles as Gentiles, alongside Jews, in one body.
7: "The gift of God's grace given me through the working of his power" — the double genitive ("gift of grace") followed by "working of power" uses two of the key-term pairs that characterize Ephesians' power-language (cf. Ephesians 1:19). Paul grounds his ministry authorization entirely in divine action, not human qualification. This is consistent with his defensive posture in Galatians and 1-2 Corinthians, where his apostolic credentials were challenged.
8: "Less than the least of all the Lord's people" — the Greek superlative of the comparative (elachistoteros) is grammatically an intensified self-deprecation. This is a recognized rhetorical topos in Greco-Roman letter-writing: establishing ethos through exaggerated humility before making a significant claim. Paul employs it also in 1 Corinthians 15:9 ("least of the apostles") and 1 Timothy 1:15 ("worst of sinners"). The convention does not undercut sincerity — it was the form in which genuine humility was expressed in elevated ancient prose.
9: "Hidden in God since the ages began" — the phrase echoes Jewish language about divine secrets concealed before creation and disclosed in the end-time (Daniel 2:22; 1 Enoch Ephesians 51:3; Wis Ephesians 6:22). The hiddenness was not arbitrary but purposeful: the fullness of the divine plan could only be disclosed at the point of its execution. The "administration" of this mystery is again the oikonomia term — Paul manages what God has now opened up for public view.
10: "The rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms" — the angelic and demonic hierarchies of Second Temple cosmology (Ephesians 1:21; Colossians 1:16; 1 Enoch 61) are the unwitting audience for the church's existence. Paul's point is that the Jew-Gentile unity of the church is not merely a sociological fact but a cosmic proclamation: the "manifold wisdom" (πολυποίκιλος σοφία) of God — many-colored, variegated — is displayed through the church's unprecedented ethnic reconciliation to the watching cosmic hierarchy.
11: "His eternal purpose accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord" — the Greek prothesis tōn aiōnōn (purpose of the ages) uses the same pre-temporal framing as Ephesians 1:4 and Ephesians 1:9. God's plan is not improvised in response to human history but executed from a design established before time. This is not fatalism — the plan required historical participants and human response — but it is a claim that the Gentile mission was no accident or afterthought.
12: "Boldness and confident access through faith in him" — parrēsia (boldness) was the right of free citizens to speak openly in the assembly — a political right of the politeuma (civic community). It was denied to slaves and foreigners. Prosagōgē (access) was the formal presentation before a monarch or high official — requiring an intermediary patron. Paul presents believers as having both: the free citizen's right of bold speech and the courtier's granted audience before the divine king — through Christ as the patron who effects the introduction.
13: "My sufferings for you, which are your glory" — in the Roman patron-client system, a patron's honor normally extended to his clients; equally, a patron's disgrace shamed them. Paul inverts this: his imprisonment (which in Roman eyes dishonors him) actually produces glory for the Gentile communities he serves. This is the logic of the cross applied to apostolic ministry: what the world reads as shame is, within God's economy, the mechanism of honor for others.
14: Kneeling was not the standard Jewish posture for prayer. Standing, with hands raised, was the norm (cf. 1 Kings 8:22; Mark 11:25; Nehemiah 8:6). Kneeling was a posture of deep urgency, penitence, or prostration before a superior (cf. Elijah in 1 Kings 18:42). Paul's deliberate note that he kneels (γονύ κάμπτω) signals that this is not routine prayer but an act of particular intensity and submission — his own posture enacting the spirit of the petition he is about to offer.
15: "From whom every family in heaven and on earth derives its name" — in the ancient world, naming was a covenantal and political act. The one who names exercises authority over what is named (Genesis 2:19-20; Ephesians 17:5). Paul's claim is that all ordered communities — whether human or angelic — derive their relational structure from the divine Father. The Greek patria (family, clan, lineage) shares its root with patēr (Father) — Paul may be exploiting this etymology: every patria is named from the Patēr.
16: "Out of his glorious riches" — the repeated "riches" language across Ephesians (Ephesians 1:7, 18; Ephesians 2:4, 7; Ephesians 3:8, 16) reflects the imagery of a great patron with vast, inexhaustible benefactions to distribute. In the Greco-Roman patronage system, the patron's generosity was measured by the abundance and continuity of his gifts; those who gave beyond what was expected were celebrated as especially beneficent. Paul applies this framework to God as the ultimate patron whose resources are immeasurable.
17: "Rooted and established in love" — Paul deploys two standard ancient metaphors for stability: agricultural (rooted, like a tree whose root system determines its resilience) and architectural (established/founded, like a building on a solid foundation). Both images appear in Hellenistic wisdom literature and Greco-Roman philosophical writing about the virtuous person who cannot be moved by circumstances. Paul's ground for this stability is love — not philosophical training or moral virtue.
18: "Wide and long and high and deep" — the four spatial dimensions echo rabbinic and Hellenistic descriptions of the scope of wisdom (Job 11:7-9; Wis Ephesians 7:24; Sir Ephesians 24:3-7) and the measurements of the heavenly temple in apocalyptic literature (Ezek 40-48; Revelation 21:16). Paul does not explain what the four dimensions measure — the point is vastness beyond measurement. The language deliberately exceeds the categories available to express it.
19: "That you may be filled to the measure of all the fullness of God" — the language of divine fullness filling the believer picks up temple imagery: the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34) and the temple (1 Kings 8:10-11; Ezekiel 43:5). The eschatological hope was that God's glory would fill all the earth (Habakkuk 2:14; Numbers 14:21). Paul applies this filling not to a building but to persons — the believer-as-temple theme of Ephesians 2:22 reaches its climactic expression here.
20: The doxology follows a Jewish prayer pattern: acknowledgment of God's incomprehensible greatness, exceeding all human petition or imagination, before the closing ascription of glory. Similar structures appear in Jewish liturgical texts (1QH; Nehemiah 9:5) and throughout the Psalms. "According to his power that is at work within us" — the same dunamis/energeia word pair from Ephesians 1:19-20, now turned inward: the resurrection power that raised Christ is the same power active inside the praying community.
21: "Throughout all generations, for ever and ever" — the Greek phrase εἰς πάσας τὰς γενεὰς τοῦ αἰῶνος τῶν αἰώνων (into all the generations of the age of ages) is an intensified version of the Jewish doxological formula l'olam va'ed (to the age and its age). The multiplication of temporal expressions in the doxology is itself an enactment of the unlimited scope it asserts — human language at its most expansive still falls short of the eternal reality it attempts to describe.
